title: Chapter 16: The Argument
content: The Arbiter raised her hand. A scroll materialized in the air — the deed to Blackwood University.

"This document," she said, "shows the transfer of ownership from the previous title holder — one Marcus Chen, who claimed authority over the campus by right of prior occupation — to Roxanne Vance, by purchase. However, Marcus Chen did not hold the original title. The university's legal ownership before the Nether-Grid's descent was held by a board of trustees. Marcus Chen was never a trustee."
She let the scroll unfurl fully.
"Therefore, the sale was invalid. Marcus Chen sold something he did not own. The campus should revert to unclaimed status."
Roxanne looked at the scroll. Then at the Arbiter.
"Your argument has a flaw," she said.
"Oh?"

"The Nether-Grid doesn't recognize human property law. When the Nether-Grid descended, all human contracts were voided. The campus's legal status was reset to 'unclaimed territory.' Marcus Chen claimed it by occupation, not inheritance. Under Nether-Grid law, occupation is a valid basis for ownership after a 72-hour period. He held the campus for approximately 48 hours before I purchased it. So technically, he didn't have clear title yet."
"Then how did you acquire clear title?"

"I didn't need him to have clear title. I bought his 'claim,' not his 'ownership.' Under Nether-Grid Contract Code, Section 3, subsection B: a claim can be transferred whether or not it has matured into full ownership. The transfer conveys the claim, not the title. My ownership matured when the 72-hour window expired — at which point I was the claim holder. So the title passed to me automatically."
She smiled.

"Check the timestamp on the deed. My purchase went through at 11:47 AM on Day 1. The 72-hour mark passed at approximately 6:47 AM on Day 4. By the time your audit arrived, I had held the claim long enough for it to mature into full ownership."
The Arbiter was silent.
She reviewed the scroll. Checked the timestamps. Checked the code reference.
Then she laughed. A real laugh, warm and surprised.
"You read the Nether-Grid Contract Code?"
"I had some time between buying a god and registering a Sovereign Account."
"In three days?"
"I'm a fast reader."
The Arbiter set down the scroll.
"Your argument is sound. The claim is valid. The campus is yours."
She extended her hand.
"Welcome to the Upper Chamber, Second Seat."
Roxanne shook it.
"Thank you."

"But I should warn you: the old man doesn't do favors. If he put you here, he needs you here. Find out why before he collects."
\n---\n
Roxanne walked out of the Hall of Settlement with a new title and a headache.
"Second Seat of the Upper Chamber," she said, testing the phrase. "That sounds like a lot."

"It is a lot," Silas said. "You now have authority over contract disputes in approximately 40% of the known Nether-Grid. You can adjudicate territorial claims. You can approve or deny soul transfers. You can veto Exchange policy decisions."
"And all I did was win a debate."
"The best victories are the ones that look easy."
They walked through the fortress's corridor of scrolls. The Arbiter's servants had already started updating the records — Roxanne's name was being inscribed into the Second Seat's ledger.
"What now?" she asked.
"Now you have a target on your back. The Arbiter had enemies. Those enemies are now your enemies. And the Third Seat's favor just became significantly more expensive."
"Because he put me in power."
"Because he demonstrated that he can put people in power. Every faction in the Exchange will now be trying to figure out who else he's placed, and what they owe him."
She stopped at the fortress gates. Looked back at the labyrinth of contracts she now controlled.
"I'm going to need a very good assistant."
"You have one."
"I need someone who understands contract law."
"I've been observing contract law for four billion years. I'll manage."
She smiled.
"Good. Then let's go home. I need to redecorate the dean's office."
The Exchange had a rhythm. Roxanne was learning to feel it.
Every transaction created a ripple. Every contract signed shifted the balance somewhere. The whole system was an enormous, interconnected web of debt and credit, and she was now a node at its center.
"We need to move faster," she said, walking through the campus. "The Council will regroup. They'll find a loophole. We need to secure more territory, more resources, more leverage before they do."
"You can't buy everything at once," Silas said.
"Watch me."
She held up the card.
*DISPLAY: ALL AVAILABLE DEBT CONTRACTS — EASTERN SECTOR.*
The screen filled with thousands of entries. Personal debts. Business debts. Soul contracts. Each with a buyout price, each representing a person or entity bound to the old system.
*TOTAL: 47,293 CONTRACTS. TOTAL BUYOUT: 12,400,000 OBOLS.*
"Purchase all."
*APPROVED. 12,400,000 OBOLS DEBITED. 47,293 CONTRACTS FORGIVEN.*
The Exchange feed updated. A wave of green icons spread across the map — debts cleared, obligations dissolved.
All across the city, people felt it. A weight lifting. A chain breaking. A sudden, inexplicable freedom.
Roxanne pocketed the card.
"That's a start."
"You found the loophole," Silas said, reading over her shoulder. "The original Charter. The First Seat's commentary. You turned the Arbiter's own library against her."

"I turned her own system against her. She's been ruling based on precedent for six thousand years. But the original Charter predates all of her precedents. If she rules against me, I can appeal directly to the First Seat with evidence that her entire tenure has been built on a misinterpretation."
"She won't let that happen."
"She can't stop it. The appeal process is automatic if a Second Seat ruling is challenged by another Second Seat holder. That's the job — we're equals."
"You've been Second Seat for less than a day and you're already planning to appeal your first case."
"I work fast."
She closed the ancient text. The Archive around her hummed with approval — or maybe that was just the ambient energy of accumulated legal history.
"Let's go back to campus. I need to tell everyone we won."
